item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as no source found indicates the first image has been released under Creative Commons, and the second image comes from here and is copyrighted. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it. +1

Also, help I can’t unsee this

https://imgur.com/a/Cxf7HHd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favourite articles on the site. I love stories with a mindboggling scale.
A first SCP reaching into the multiple hundreds. That's not something you see everyday.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved reading at the containment protocols where it address the need to handle extraterrestrial influence. I hadn't read the rest of the article and so I envisioned some agents in nice suits and sunglasses throwing amnestic pills at a UFO

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i think that this one is good but i did read some comment that said that it should not be keter i disagree with this fully i mean something that could rearrange the cosmos could have many different types of astrological effects that could very easy have a xk class end result think of a quasar being put into our solar system or redirecting a gamma ray burst at the planet also great job for a first upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely amazing. One of the first I read, and also super-original as well as ominous. +1 to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an astrophysics student (even one who still has just had a basic introduction to cosmology) I love this - especially the part about how the foundation decided there was one way it worked, and they misdirect any research showing results beyond their explanation.

How I assume this works is that the tachyons go back in time to the stage right before the great expansion (where, according to theory, the constant vacuum fluctuations suddenly become much bigger made the mass/energy distribution of the universe uneven) and organizing the fluctuations in patterns, causing the mass to distribute itself through the universe in such a way that the galaxies are formed according to the designs in the box. A very fun idea, which gives this my (as a new user) very first +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It turns out that SCP-2070-1 actually points at the dick of [DATA REDACTED]

item.title: Re: I know why this is a Keter-class
item.content:encoded: Um… I'm pretty sure the article establishes that we're the ones moving galaxies. :P

item.title: I know why this is a Keter-class
item.content:encoded: Think about it for one moment. Someone, or something, is moving galaxies just to send messages to us, and oh golly are they not peaceful messages! If something this strong is sending messages to us, something that no weapon on our Earth can destroy, something stronger than SCP-682 and SCP-2399 combined, we should be very wary. (I know SCPs aren't real just to let you know)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, they tried beating the methodology out of him, but he had a weak heart and died. And even if you had the methodology, it wouldn't work if anyone other than him built it.

(And please note that I'm not the one who wrote this SCP!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
actually, the way you put it makes the fear exhibited even more irrational. If the chance of found is so beyond infinitesimal, why is there a need for the Foundation to Institute 167-Tully?

Also, I don't think any lines formed by this scp will even remain a line when viewed from another galaxy. My general relativity knowledge is admittedly a bit rusty but wouldn't the expansion of the universe distort it due to the apparent motion being relative to the distances between the galaxies?

also, during the Shang dynasty and throughout Chinese history, astronomy was always a state enterprise since it was believed the ruler must commune with the heavens. If some artificer figured out how to do such a thing, he wouldn't be able to keep it quiet. Either he'd manipulat e the heavens to put himself on the throne, or somebody is going to beat the methodology out of him.

I think it would be better to make it not of Shang origin but discovered by them, and that its abilities are narrowly tailored so they can't militarize it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Shang astronomers couldn't manipulate time and space. They had one anomalous artificer, he made the box for them, and then he died.

Also, I still prefer the idea that this thing doesn't manipulate clusters of galaxies. It manipulates the images of clusters of galaxies as seen from Earth.

Also, remember that these don't point to Earth. They point to our galaxy, which contains ~ 4*1011 stars. If only 1% of 1% of stars are in systems that look like ours, that'd still be ~40 million.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think my concern with this one is actually the fear of it being discovered by aliens.

Suppose we have an alien observer on another galaxy looking at the galaxy distribution, unless the Shang astronomers also nuked relativity and expanding universe, it will appear nothing like what it would be seen on Earth.

Even if they thought the distribution was off, I don't think they can reconstruct any arrows without knowing beforehand what the frame of reference was.

Also, just throwing this out there, but if the Shang astronomers can already manipulate time and space, how did their empire fall?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I figgure that if your gonna make a device that retroactively delcares your planet the most important place in the universe, you will want to ensure that said planet survives the change in its original state. It wouldn't do to overwrite the very place you're trying to promote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does it really make sense that aliens would believe that a species that has the power to do something like this, and obviously isn't afraid of bragging about it while revealing their location, would think Earth was ripe for invasion? If anything, they'd either be terrified and stay the fuck away, or come here on pilgrimages to worship us like gods and ask for ultimate knowledge of the universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never thought about that aspect of it.

Well, it's not our fault. We're keeping it under lock and key so that no one uses it again; would be kind of silly to go after the entire Earth for something done so long ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: considering the emergency protocol specifically states alien knowledge of the artifact as grounds to activation rather than simply alien knowledge of earth, I would theorize that the keter classification is not just because it's a map to us, but because during the process of shuffling the entire universe just to suit our egos, we may have stepped on quite a few alien toes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since the object emits tachyons, it's assumed it moves galaxies by causing changes in the past perhaps very far in the past, like making minute changes to the state of the universe soon after the big bang so that galaxies form in this predetermined shape in the future. Because of that, restoring the galaxies wont annoy them because they would never remember the previous orientation, this would just be how the galaxies have always been. That being said, this raises the obvious question of how WE know this thing is changing galaxies since in theory our own memories wouldn't survive this change either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Depends on exactly how badly our reputation is destroyed. We don't know how this thing affects galaxies on a small scale. It's shuffling the whole universe around, it's likely that it doesn't get the locations of the smaller objects EXACTLY right each time. Maybe the relative locations of things aren't preserved. Maybe solar systems and planets end up shifted ever-so-slightly, but enough to perhaps cause catastrophic disasters, wipe out entire civilizations, and maybe cause some to never even develop in the first place. Basically I'm saying when the aliens discover we have this thing, they may have good reason to be PISSED.

I think this is supported by the fact that conditions to activate the safety protocol isn't just aliens discovering the location of earth, but "extraterrestrial knowledge of SCP-2070".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's (roughly) translated from some ancient Shang-dynasty script so obviously the wording isn't going to be the best. Given that Chinese represents entire ideas with single characters, a short phrase that sounds like it came from a five year old in English translation could very well be considered eloquent in the original language. Besides, coming from the guys who literally named their country "The Center", the phrases aren't much of a stretch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everyone is concerned that aliens might find us trough this, meh.

I'm more concerned about Dr. Bright using this to draw a giant dickbutt in space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was really liking it until I saw the third piece of the SCP. Some god box? I can kinda forgive asking astronomers to play nice and not tell the world that ancient Chinese made a galactic autobahn. Kinda.

totally drown out anyone

It might be just me not having read as many dossiers as I would like, but I've not seen containment procedures or their authors to be prone to hyperbole of any kind.

As for the box, wanting me to believe it vanished after so long is a little much for me to believe. I don't see it being lost to time in the slightest when it is something of this scale. If it was buried alongside of some great emperor or king, possibly. It feels like this needs a "buried alongside the tomb of [REDACTED]" This is just asking too much.

As for the box, it's effects seem relatively benign. Yes, it rewrites the galactic order and the balance of matter in the universe, but that's not touched upon in the slightest. Nor does it discuss all the extraterrestrials out there who now have a road map towards earth. There's plenty of reason for it to be a Keter, but as it stands, this entry doesn't have any teeth. Neutral vote.

item.title: Genius
item.content:encoded: I really like how you used tachyons to make this work. Its great when sci-fi builds on existing science, which you don't often see here, especially not done this well. Superb. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Prometheus, anyone? Except not, because WE ARE THE PROMETHEANS.

This is really, really good, and it gets me really fascinated and creeps me out simultaneously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm thinking it's a Keter because the possibility that extraterrestrial beings having already landed on Earth and are within our populations since the the box was used for the first time.

It's a paranoia case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I though it was really interesting. I think It played at the possibility of ancient reality benders, which is pretty cool.
Up vote!
but I think it should have been Euclid. A map of the milky way isn't Keter. It's freaky Euclid stuff going down, but not Keter. Iagree that astick figure isn't very conclusive. Keter refers to containment, mostly. Containment isn't that complex. Besides, if ETs can fly to use, they would probably notice us anyway. Keter atop a document is a good first impression, but this was a Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And that is why no one wants to talk to us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Impressive.
That's first SCP I have read is related to ancient China but not Qin Dynasty.
I give upvote for that.

But -3 is too powerful to a box, I don't like it so much. It may more convincing if it is a ritual or sth but not a galaxy mover box.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, this is your first SCP? Look at you, all established and stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They might not have made the connection that the symbols are meaningful/point to us. The only reason we know for sure is we have a magical space-mover.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for the sheer scale of the skip, and the clever use of real-life creepy phenomena. For me, that withstands the few loose ends.

I'm curious, though: The procedures seem to indicate that the Foundation is aware of extraterrestrials on Earth already, but that those beings are currently unaware of the anomalous arrangement of everything. How is it possible that we know about it, but ETs (who presumably traveled here and therefore exceed our spacefaring capability) don't?

Also, why do we imagine that restoring galaxies to homogeneity wouldn't annoy the rest of universe even more? Maybe they like it this way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Update 2014-4-11: Overhauled containment procedures.

item.title: Re: It's up!
item.content:encoded: Update 2014-4-11: Overhauled containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is huge and cosmic and fucking cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Weird thought, but I can't help but shake the feeling that seeing what would happen if AWCY? got their hands on this thing would be… interesting.

Also, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, thanks for clarifying that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, it's a giant roadmap to the Milky Way Galaxy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry if I'm slow, but it's keter because it's a giant roadmap to earth for every and any ET being, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite the innate ridiculousness of the containment procedures I feel compelled to upvote. You just have to respect the solid steel balls of the Shang astronomers. They found out they weren't the center of the universe and responded by telling the universe to suck it long and hard. With tachyons. How you like them apples causality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with both the Conservation of WTF and the ridiculousness of the procedures, having read them again. Going around and asking politely for all the astronomers in the world to cooperate is something that is completely unbelievable, even in the realm of fantasy.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This exceeds my personal Conservation of WTF threshold by a huge margin. Leaving aside the absolute ridiculousness of the containment procedures, which would pretty much guarantee that this information gets leaked, assuming that no amateur astronomers would notice entire galaxy clusters arranged in the described manner, this is just too much.

This essentially requires the creators of this skip to be God, and I have a hard time believing that something that's attained that much power is going to use it for such a completely asinine purpose. And then mysteriously vanish, leaving the God Box buried in a hole somewhere for some reason. And then of course the Foundation deems the wisest course of action to be fucking with the thing that causes retrocausal readjustment of the entire goddamn universe. Nope.

Also, my impression is that we're supposed to take this article seriously. If that's the case…

Rough translations of messages include "WE ARE KINGS", "WE ARE SPECIAL", and "WE ARE THE CENTER"

Jesus, really? These guys attain the goddamn power to change the SKY and that's what they come up with? Are they going to come back and carve "LOL EAT AT JOE'S" into the moon?

There's the elements of something really good here, but it's weighed down by just way too much ridiculousness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone who absolutely loved astronomy growing up, I really enjoyed this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Which is pretty much what happened, I suppose.

Yeah, look forward to seeing more from you, mate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "What a bunch of morons! They found out the universe is unthinkably infinite and threw a temper tantrum about it!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also like this SCP.

Except, I bet this wouldn't destroy our planet, just our cosmic reputation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this answer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Regarding your first point: it certainly could be that the device only reshapes what we see, but I would think that the tachyon emission constrains that possibility somewhat. If you wanted to alter EM waves there are more efficient ways to do that, and tachyons are tied in sci-fi narrative to time shenanigans.

Regarding your second point: of course the aliens aren't all going to be able to uniquely identify us from this SCP, which is why we haven't already started looking for a way to undo it. But the SCP does leave a lot of clues which, taken together, help to identify us - maps of the solar system, of the solar neighborhood, of the Milky Way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's more likely, and easier: that these Shang-era astronomers were able to reshape the universe but only did it on a scale of billions of galaxies? Or that they reshaped the images of galaxies as they're seen from Earth ?

Heinlein — I think it was Heinlein — did a story about this, actually. Astronomers realize that the brightest stars in the sky are all moving anomalously fast in weird directions, and after several days they're horrified to see that the brightest stars in the sky have rearranged themselves to spell the words "BUY SNIVLEY'S SOAP".

And in his apartment, George Sniveley looks up at the sky, cackling over his success of his machine, and then realizes IT'S SPELLED WRONG, and has a heart attack out of sheer rage and frustration.

Three months later, the power company notices that he hasn't been paying his bill (because he's dead), and shuts off his electricity. Once the machine shuts off, the stars instantly switch back to their original positions. The actual stars hadn't moved, just their images as seen from Earth.

(but for those three months, Sniveley's Soap was the top-selling soap in the whole world.)

Even if we do assume that this is actually reshaping the positions of the galaxies instead of just their images, remember that most of our galaxy would have the same vantage point of the modifications as we do. The Zyglaxians would think "oh, those arrows point to Zyglax! And look, there's a stick-figure image of a Zyglaxian - sort of crude, and the antlers are too small, but it's obviously intentional! And an image of Zyglax Prime and its ten planets!"

Entities in nearby galaxies would also have a similar vantage point. And if you've never seen a humanoid bauplan, you won't be able to recognize one when you see a crude stick-figure image of one spelled out in billions of galaxies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. It's likely 100% because I can sympathize with those Shang astronomers' reasons for creating the box. I just love all the implications in this.

I will mention that the clearance-blocked part of the containment came off a bit like Men in Black to me, but maybe that wasn't intended. I don't think it took away from the article as a whole, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it, but yeah, these reasons are in effect for me as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a very close downvote for me. This is a superb attempt at a first SCP, and very commendable. The tone was excellent, and I think the idea was kind of cool. Ultimately, it came down to the execution and the ending twist.

My quibbles with it:

You blackbox names in the Special Containment Procedures. This is kind of counterproductive, because then nobody knows where this thing is, or where to consult the right people to handle the situation.

The next thing is personal in nature, so take it with a grain of salt. I personally feel that Level 2 is a little low in terms of clearance, as this would include practically all research staff, which kind of negates the value of the SCP being top secret.

Back to less subjective stuff. The initial circumstances of -3's recovery seem a little iffy to me. I think that this box having weird drawings is a little tenuous at best for Foundation containment. This kind of plays into the rest of the article, because I'm left considering how in the heck someone decided that this weird looking box had to be contained.

My biggest issue boils down to the final note. At times, the researcher seems slightly melodramatic, especially in the last sentence. It adds this air of melodrama and fanciful language that really contrasts with the rest of the article, and the note. This is present at other points in the note as well.

I'm also a bit mixed with how you finished it off. I think that listing the aspects of the procedure really hurt the prior effect of the "Whoa, this is an ancient galaxy mover." You have this great twist moment with the revelation of the box, but then the article seems to drag on for another gasp through the procedures, leaving the reader with an overall mixed reception.

All in all, this was a great attempt. It just personally didn't work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it for the theme. Liked it a lot.

A lot.

item.title: It's up!
item.content:encoded: Thanks to everyone who had encouraging words. Thanks to HatMan and Soulless for their sharp editing advice, and thanks especially to Aelanna for getting me to think about whether I even wanted an article like this on the site.
